explanation: The natural language requirement states that the controller should be able to implement (support) the listed six modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff (which appears to correspond with StandbyMode), Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode (PCV), Pressure Support Ventilation Mode (PSV), and fail-safe. An appropriate LTL property for this would ensure that it is always possible to eventually reach each of those modes, i.e., none of these modes are unreachable. Based on the provided atomic propositions, we have 'StartUpMode', 'SelfTestMode', 'StandbyMode' (VentilationOff), 'PCVMode', 'PSVMode', and 'failSafeMode' representing these states. The LTL formula thus asserts that it is always the case that, for each mode, it is possible to eventually enter that mode (i.e., G(F(mode))). This guarantees the system is not locked out of any required mode. If we just required (F mode_i), it would allow a run in which each mode is entered only once; by using G(F(mode)), we require the ability to reach each mode always in the future (none are dead).
output_LTL: G(F(StartUpMode)) & G(F(SelfTestMode)) & G(F(StandbyMode)) & G(F(PCVMode)) & G(F(PSVMode)) & G(F(failSafeMode))

explanation:
The input natural language specifies that the controller shall implement six operational modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff (Standby Mode), Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode (PCV), Pressure Support Ventilation Mode (PSV), and Fail-safe Mode. In the atomic propositions, each of these modes is represented as an atomic proposition: StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode (as VentilationOff), PCVMode, PSVMode, and failSafeMode. The intent is that, at all times, the controller is in one of these modes, and these modes are mutually exclusive. In LTL, this is captured by stating that globally (G), exactly one of these mode atomic propositions must hold at any time (exclusive OR, represented as a conjunction where for each i, if mode i is true, all others are false, and at least one is always true).

No numerical comparisons are used, only atomic propositions and Boolean operators as required. Prior possible translations may have omitted the mutual exclusivity or the requirement that one mode is always active; this formula captures both for all times.

LTL property: G ((StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode) & !(StartUpMode & SelfTestMode) & !(StartUpMode & StandbyMode) & !(StartUpMode & PCVMode) & !(StartUpMode & PSVMode) & !(StartUpMode & failSafeMode) & !(SelfTestMode & StandbyMode) & !(SelfTestMode & PCVMode) & !(SelfTestMode & PSVMode) & !(SelfTestMode & failSafeMode) & !(StandbyMode & PCVMode) & !(StandbyMode & PSVMode) & !(StandbyMode & failSafeMode) & !(PCVMode & PSVMode) & !(PCVMode & failSafeMode) & !(PSVMode & failSafeMode))
output_LTL: G ((StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode) & !(StartUpMode & SelfTestMode) & !(StartUpMode & StandbyMode) & !(StartUpMode & PCVMode) & !(StartUpMode & PSVMode) & !(StartUpMode & failSafeMode) & !(SelfTestMode & StandbyMode) & !(SelfTestMode & PCVMode) & !(SelfTestMode & PSVMode) & !(SelfTestMode & failSafeMode) & !(StandbyMode & PCVMode) & !(StandbyMode & PSVMode) & !(StandbyMode & failSafeMode) & !(PCVMode & PSVMode) & !(PCVMode & failSafeMode) & !(PSVMode & failSafeMode))

explanation: The input natural language states that the controller should implement a set of distinct operational modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, Ventilation Off/Standby (VentilationOff), Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode, Pressure Support Ventilation Mode, and Fail-Safe Mode. There are corresponding atomic propositions for each of these: StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode (which the dictionary says is equivalent to VentilationOff), PCVMode, PSVMode, and failSafeMode. To capture the requirement that the controller implements these modes, we express that it is always the case (globally, using G) that it is possible for the system to eventually (F) reach each of these modes. This does not enforce exclusivity, ordering, or minimal switching logic, but simply that all these modes are implemented and reachable at some point in any system run. A naïve translation would be a big conjunction of 'GF mode' for each mode. This is a common and correct LTL pattern for 'implement these modes' where we don't have details on mode transitions or mutual exclusivity.
output_LTL: G(F StartUpMode) & G(F SelfTestMode) & G(F StandbyMode) & G(F PCVMode) & G(F PSVMode) & G(F failSafeMode)

explanation: The input requires the controller to implement (i.e., be able to enter) several defined operating modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff (StandbyMode), Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode (PCVMode), Pressure Support Ventilation Mode (PSVMode), and fail-safe (failSafeMode). Since LTL cannot express reachability of multiple modes with exclusive transitions without dedicated transition variables, and given the provided atomic propositions which indicate these modes being active, the best translation is to require that it is always possible (eventually, from any state) to reach each of the required modes. This is captured by conjunctions of 'GF mode' for each required mode; that is, 'Globally, it is always the case that, eventually, the mode will be (re-)entered.' This covers that all listed modes are implemented and accessible (not that they must all be active at once, which is not required for controller modes).
output_LTL: GF StartUpMode & GF SelfTestMode & GF StandbyMode & GF PCVMode & GF PSVMode & GF failSafeMode

explanation: The natural language requirement specifies that the controller must implement (i.e., be able to enter) six distinct modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff (Standby Mode), Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode (PCV), Pressure Support Ventilation Mode (PSV), and Fail-safe mode. Given the available atomic propositions, we must capture that these modes must be (at some point) possible to occur. The best approximation using the given atomic propositions is to require that, at some point in execution, each mode's atomic proposition becomes true at least once. This is naturally expressed in LTL by stating that each atomic proposition is eventually true (F p), and by conjoining these for all the relevant modes. More advanced properties expressing mutual exclusivity or full mode switching logics are not required by the input sentence and would be under-specified given only what is present in the atomic set. Thus, the LTL output is the conjunction (AND) of "eventually"-subformulas for StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode (for VentilationOff), PCVMode, PSVMode, and failSafeMode. Other LTL properties (e.g., using 'G' or exclusions) would not match the intent, as this is about existence (implementing) and not invariance or safety between modes.
output_LTL: F StartUpMode & F SelfTestMode & F StandbyMode & F PCVMode & F PSVMode & F failSafeMode

explanation: The input natural language requires that the controller implements (i.e., supports and can enter) several modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff (Standby), Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode (PCV), Pressure Support Ventilation Mode (PSV), and Fail-safe. In the context of LTL, the most straightforward way to capture 'the system shall implement the following modes' is to require that it is always possible for the system to eventually enter each of these modes. This is best captured using 'GF mode', meaning 'infinitely often, it is possible to reach this mode' (the system does not deadlock in a state where the mode cannot be entered anymore). Since LTL does not have a direct way to specify system capability, the standard way is to require each mode is eventually entered at least once (F mode), or (if stronger) that it can be visited again and again (GF mode). For completeness and strict interpretation, we use 'FG mode' for each, i.e., along all paths, eventually the mode will be entered. Therefore, we list that eventually, each relevant mode state is entered: StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode, PCVMode, PSVMode, and failSafeMode. Since the natural language sentences list the names of the modes and expects the controller to implement all of them, the LTL property combines all with conjunction (&): eventually StartUpMode is true, and eventually SelfTestMode is true, and so on for each listed mode.
output_LTL: F StartUpMode & F SelfTestMode & F StandbyMode & F PCVMode & F PSVMode & F failSafeMode

explanation:
The input sentence requires that the controller implements (i.e., is capable of entering or supporting) a set of distinct operational modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff (StandbyMode), Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode (PCVMode), Pressure Support Ventilation Mode (PSVMode), and Fail-safe Mode (failSafeMode). LTL formulas cannot directly express the presence of mutually exclusive modes or enumerate a mode list, but we can represent the requirement as: 'Eventually, each mode must become active at least once.' This is captured by the LTL 'F' (eventually) operator applied to each atomic proposition corresponding to the required modes.

Thus, the formula is the conjunction of five 'eventually' properties, one for each identified mode. We use StandbyMode for 'VentilationOff,' as per the description, and include all others explicitly. This guarantees that at some point during any execution, each required mode will be entered, which aligns with the interpretation that the controller 'implements' these operational modes. It does not, however, specify persistence, order, or mutual exclusion, but is the most likely LTL translation given the APs and the typical use of LTL for capability requirements.
output_LTL: F StartUpMode & F SelfTestMode & F StandbyMode & F PCVMode & F PSVMode & F failSafeMode

explanation: The natural language requirement states that the controller must implement (i.e., support or allow operation in) a set of named modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff, Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode, Pressure Support Ventilation Mode, and fail-safe. In LTL, to express that each of these modes must be possible (i.e., the system must, at some point, either initially or in the future, provide an operation context in which each of these named modes is available/entered), we require that, globally, eventually, each of the mode indicators becomes true (i.e., the system enters each mode at least once eventually in operation). This is formalized as: G (F StartUpMode) & G (F SelfTestMode) & G (F StandbyMode) & G (F PCVMode) & G (F PSVMode) & G (F failSafeMode). Here, StandbyMode is used as the closest available atomic proposition for 'VentilationOff' due to its dictionary description. This LTL expresses that, throughout any possible execution or run of the system, each mode must be entered at least once eventually (i.e., these modes are implemented in a strong sense: they can all become active as appropriate). Other possible LTL formulas that only reference one mode or do not combine them conjunctively would fail to capture the requirement that all the specified modes be implemented, not just one or some subset. This formulation does not (and cannot, given only Boolean indicators and no numerical or calendar constraints) guarantee correct sequencing, mutual exclusivity, or specific transitions between modes, but it does encode that all are reachable/implementable as required.
output_LTL: G (F StartUpMode) & G (F SelfTestMode) & G (F StandbyMode) & G (F PCVMode) & G (F PSVMode) & G (F failSafeMode)

explanation: The natural language requires that the controller implements (i.e., is able to enter) six distinct modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff, Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode, Pressure Support Ventilation Mode, and fail-safe. In the provided atomic propositions, these correspond to: StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode (as VentilationOff), PCVMode, PSVMode, and failSafeMode. The LTL property 'G (StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode)' ensures that globally (i.e., at all times), the controller is always in at least one of these six modes, ensuring there is always a mode corresponding to the required ones active. This captures the requirement that the controller implements these modes by asserting that it is always in one of them. Previous LTLs that may, for example, individually assert only the existence or reachability (using eventually F) of each mode would not enforce continuous, mutually covered operation in these modes, whereas the global disjunction ensures no time is spent outside of the defined set.
output_LTL: G (StartUpMode | SelfTestMode | StandbyMode | PCVMode | PSVMode | failSafeMode)

explanation: The requirement asks that the controller shall implement (i.e., have, be able to enter) several modes: Start-up Mode, Self-Test Mode, VentilationOff (Standby), Pressure Controlled Ventilation Mode, Pressure Support Ventilation Mode, and Fail-safe. In LTL, this is best expressed by stating that, at least once during system operation, each of these modes is entered (i.e., the atomic proposition for that mode becomes true at least once). The relevant atomic propositions are StartUpMode, SelfTestMode, StandbyMode (for VentilationOff), PCVMode, PSVMode, and failSafeMode. The most direct encoding of this requirement is a conjunction of 'eventually' (F) statements for each mode: F(mode), anded together. This ensures that the system can, at any point in time, enter each of these modes at least once. Previous LTLs that do not require eventual entry into all these modes would be insufficient, as mere existence of the mode variables or unrelated behavior is NOT enough—the system must be able to enter each mode at some point.
output_LTL: F(StartUpMode) & F(SelfTestMode) & F(StandbyMode) & F(PCVMode) & F(PSVMode) & F(failSafeMode)
